description:
You are acting as a QA automation engineer who has just received a small set of JSON
    fixtures together with a JSON-Schema.  
    The goal is to automate the validation of the fixtures and to extract a
    concise list of e-mail addresses that belong to all **schema-compliant**
    users.

    1. Directory layout that already exists  
       • /home/user/qa/user_schema.json – the official schema  
       • /home/user/qa/json_samples/user1.json  
       • /home/user/qa/json_samples/user2.json  
       • /home/user/qa/json_samples/user3.json  

       The contents of those files are *already* present; you do **not** have to
       create them.  You may open and inspect them to understand the
       requirements.

    2. What you have to build  
       a) Create a helper directory:  
          /home/user/qa/scripts/  

       b) Inside that directory write a Bash script named
          validate_users.sh that performs **all** of the following when it is
          executed from any location:

          • Iterates over every *.json file that is directly inside
            /home/user/qa/json_samples/ (no recursion).  
          • Validates each file against /home/user/qa/user_schema.json.  
            – You may choose any validation mechanism you like
              (python3 + jsonschema, ajv-cli, etc.).  
              The script **must** exit with code 0 even when invalid
              documents are found; the result should be communicated only in
              the log file.  
          • Creates the directory /home/user/qa/logs/ (if it does not exist)
            and writes /home/user/qa/logs/validation_report.txt  
            in the exact format given in section 3.  
          • With the help of jq (and only jq) pulls the ".email" field from
            every *valid* JSON file, sorts the resulting list in ascending
            lexical order and writes it to  
            /home/user/qa/output/emails.txt  
            (create the directory /home/user/qa/output/ if needed).  

          Ensure the script has the executable bit set
          (chmod + x /home/user/qa/scripts/validate_users.sh).

    3. Log-file specification – **must match exactly**  

       /home/user/qa/logs/validation_report.txt has to contain **one line per
       processed file** in *alphabetical* order by file name.
       The grammar of each line is:

           <filename>: VALID
           <filename>: INVALID - <human-readable-error-list>

       • The literal strings “VALID” and “INVALID” are uppercase.  
       • For an invalid file the human-readable-error-list is a
         semicolon-separated, single-line list of individual error messages.  
       • There is a single newline (LF) after every line, including the last
         one.

       Example of the correct overall file structure (the concrete messages
       will obviously depend on the validator you choose, but the file names,
       keywords and separator characters **must** match):

           user1.json: VALID
           user2.json: INVALID - id: 0 is less than the minimum of 1; email: 'not-an-email' is not a 'email'
           user3.json: VALID

    4. Specification for the e-mail list  

       /home/user/qa/output/emails.txt contains **only** the e-mail addresses
       of the JSON fixtures that were reported as “VALID” in section 3,
       sorted in ascending lexical order, one address per line, and a trailing
       newline after the last address.  Example:

           alice@example.com
           charlie@example.com

    5. What will be tested automatically  
       • Executability of /home/user/qa/scripts/validate_users.sh  
       • Exact existence and byte-for-byte contents of  
         /home/user/qa/logs/validation_report.txt  
       • Exact existence and byte-for-byte contents of  
         /home/user/qa/output/emails.txt  
       • That your script exits with status 0.

    6. Constraints  
       • No root privileges are available – user-local pip installs are fine.  
       • Do not rename or move any of the pre-supplied files.  
       • Do not hard-code absolute paths inside the validation logic; derive
         them or use variables so the script can be launched from anywhere.  
       • The solution must work on a vanilla Ubuntu-like environment with only
         common developer tools pre-installed (bash, coreutils, python3, jq,
         pip, etc.).

    7. Deliverables you must create  
       • /home/user/qa/scripts/validate_users.sh (executable)  
       • /home/user/qa/logs/validation_report.txt (exact format!)  
       • /home/user/qa/output/emails.txt (exact format!)

    When you are done, run the script at least once so that the two output
    files are actually present and can be inspected by the test harness.
truth:
<!--  Ground-truth data for automatic verification               -->
    <!--  These files already exist before the agent begins work.    -->
    <!--  The agent must *NOT* see anything inside this block.       -->

    <initial_files>
        <file path="/home/user/qa/user_schema.json"><![CDATA[
{
  "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#",
  "title": "User",
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["id", "name", "email", "roles"],
  "properties": {
    "id":    { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1 },
    "name":  { "type": "string",  "minLength": 1 },
    "email": { "type": "string",  "format": "email" },
    "roles": { "type": "array",   "items": { "type": "string" } }
  }
}
        ]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/qa/json_samples/user1.json"><![CDATA[
{"id":1,"name":"Alice","email":"alice@example.com","roles":["admin","qa"]}
        ]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/qa/json_samples/user2.json"><![CDATA[
{"id":0,"name":"Bob","email":"not-an-email","roles":[]}
        ]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/qa/json_samples/user3.json"><![CDATA[
{"id":2,"name":"Charlie","email":"charlie@example.com","roles":["dev"]}
        ]]></file>
    </initial_files>

    <expected_outputs>
        <file path="/home/user/qa/logs/validation_report.txt"><![CDATA[
user1.json: VALID
user2.json: INVALID - id: 0 is less than the minimum of 1; email: 'not-an-email' is not a 'email'
user3.json: VALID
        ]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/qa/output/emails.txt"><![CDATA[
alice@example.com
charlie@example.com
        ]]></file>
    </expected_outputs>
